reasoning: ADCs are evaluated under 3A001.a.5.a. For resolution >16 bit (here 24-bit), control requires a sample/output rate exceeding 65 MSPS (3A001.a.5.a.5). This device tops out at 256 kSPS (0.256 MSPS), far below the threshold, so it does not meet any 3A001 ADC parameter and is not otherwise listed on the CCL—classified EAR99.

item_name: Quad RF-DAC / quad RF-ADC mixed-signal front-end
reasoning: The device integrates high-speed data converters controlled under 3A001.a.5: the 4 GSPS RF-ADC channels exceed the analog-to-digital converter sample-rate thresholds of 3A001.a.5.a, and the 12 GSPS RF-DAC channels (12-bit, >1200 MSPS update rate) exceed the digital-to-analog converter thresholds of 3A001.a.5.b. Category 3 (electronics) microcircuits.

item_name: 6 GSPS RF digital-to-analog converter, to 5 GHz
reasoning: The item is a digital-to-analog converter (DAC). Under 3A001.a.5, DACs are controlled; subparagraph .c covers DACs with a resolution of 16 bits or more and a sample (update) rate greater than 1.25 GSPS. This 16-bit DAC operates at up to 6 GSPS, exceeding the 1.25 GSPS threshold, placing it under 3A001.a.5.c.

item_name: Wideband 2x2 RF agile transceiver, 47 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: This is a commercial software-defined-radio/wireless-infrastructure RF agile transceiver IC (e.g., AD9361-class) integrating front end, baseband data converters, and synthesizers. While Category 3 (3A001) covers electronic components, the integrated converters (12-bit class), 56 MHz channel bandwidth, and 47 MHz-6 GHz tuning do not meet the specific performance thresholds in 3A001.a.5 (ADC/DAC) or 3A001.b.4 (frequency synthesizer switching/bandwidth) parameters, and it is not a finished telecom system under 5A001. As a mass-market commercial part subject to the EAR but not described by a specific CCL entry, it is EAR99.

A 300 MHz single-core 32-bit audio DSP for consumer/commercial audio mixing, filtering and equalization does not meet the high-performance microprocessor or DSP thresholds in Category 3 (3A001.a.3/3A991). General-purpose audio signal processors of this modest performance are not described by any CCL entry and fall under EAR99.

item_name: Narrow-band sub-1 GHz ISM RF transceiver
reasoning: Item is a commercial low-power narrow-band sub-1 GHz ISM RF transceiver using simple FSK/GFSK modulation for wireless data links and metering. It is telecommunications transmission equipment not meeting the specific performance/spread-spectrum thresholds of 5A001, falling under the catch-all telecommunications entry 5A991.

item_name: Integrated dual-channel RF transceiver, 75 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: The device is a monolithic RF/microwave integrated circuit (transceiver assembly) with transmit/receive/observation channels and on-chip synthesizers operating up to 6 GHz, i.e. above the ~2.7 GHz microwave threshold, placing it among the microwave solid-state amplifiers/assemblies and frequency-agile components controlled under Category 3 electronics (3A001.b microwave items). If the specific instantaneous bandwidth/power parameters fall below the control thresholds it would instead be 5A991/EAR99, but the wideband, agile microwave function points to 3A001.b.

The item is a commercial integrated-circuit microprocessor/DSP system-on-chip. It does not meet the high-performance, radiation-hardened, or specialized thresholds of 3A001.a.3 (microprocessor/microcircuit controls). Such general-purpose microprocessor and DSP microcircuits fall to 3A991.a.1, which covers microprocessor and microcomputer microcircuits not controlled by 3A001.

This is an information security commodity employing cryptography: it implements symmetric AES and asymmetric ECDSA/ECDH public-key engines for data confidentiality (TLS/secure-boot acceleration), with secure key storage and a TRNG. Such hardware components designed/modified to use cryptography for confidentiality (symmetric >56-bit and asymmetric public-key) are classified under Category 5 Part 2, ECCN 5A002.a.

item_name: 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi + Bluetooth module, 4 MB flash
reasoning: This is a standard 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11) and Bluetooth wireless transceiver module providing short-range RF communications. As a radio-frequency communications device, it falls under Category 5 Part 1 (telecommunications). It is a mass-market consumer-type wireless connectivity module and does not implement controlled cryptographic functionality beyond standard ancillary use, classifying it under 5A992.c as telecommunications equipment not controlled under 5A002.

A field-programmable gate array (FPGA) is an integrated circuit. This is a general-purpose, commercial low-cost FPGA without specialized features that would meet the higher-performance controls of 3A001.a.7 (programmable logic devices). As a non-microprocessor/non-specially-designed integrated circuit not meeting 3A001 thresholds, it falls under 3A991.d covering integrated circuits not specified elsewhere.

The secure element implements cryptography for data confidentiality using AES-128 (symmetric algorithm > 56 bits) and ECDH/ECDSA elliptic-curve algorithms, meeting the criteria of Category 5 Part 2 (information security). Items designed to use 'cryptography for data confidentiality' with described security algorithms exceeding the thresholds in Note to 5A002.a fall under 5A002.a. Secure boot/authentication functions still rely on the controlled confidentiality cryptographic capability provided by the IC.

A general-purpose 16-bit microcontroller/DSC running at 60 MIPS with on-chip flash, RAM, DSP engine, USB, CAN and DMA for motor control/embedded use does not meet the technical thresholds of Category 3 microprocessor/integrated-circuit controls (e.g., 3A001.a.7 ADC/DAC, or specialized high-performance/radiation-hardened/processing aggregate criteria). Such commodity microcontrollers are not described elsewhere on the CCL and are classified EAR99.

This is a standard Layer-2 managed Ethernet switch supporting 10/100 Mbps. As telecommunications networking equipment not meeting the higher-performance or cryptographic thresholds of 5A002 (no controlled crypto specified), it falls under the general telecommunications catch-all 5A991, specifically .b.4 covering switching equipment. The basic switching/PHY functionality does not rise to 5A001 performance levels, placing it in the anti-terrorism-controlled 5A991 entry.

A standard 10/100 Ethernet PHY transceiver compliant with IEEE 802.3 is commodity networking interface hardware. It does not meet the parameters of Category 5 Part 1 telecom controls (e.g., not employing controlled coding/modulation, high-speed >100 Gbaud aggregate, or specialized features) nor any 3A integrated-circuit performance thresholds. Such mass-market commercial PHY ICs are not described by any CCL entry and fall under EAR99.

A standard commercial 10/100 Mbps Ethernet PHY transceiver is a common networking component operating well below any performance thresholds in Category 5 Part 2 (telecommunications). Basic Ethernet PHYs with RMII interfaces and cable diagnostics are not specified in 5A991 or other CCL entries, so they fall under EAR99 as items subject to the EAR but not listed on the CCL.

The MCU integrates a hardware cryptographic engine implementing symmetric algorithms (AES, 3DES) with key lengths exceeding 56 bits and supports a 'cryptographic function' using symmetric algorithms. Under Category 5 Part 2, items designed or modified to use cryptography employing symmetric algorithms with key length over 56 bits (AES/3DES) for data confidentiality are controlled under 5A002.a. The hashing (SHA) and RNG support this. The general-purpose microcontroller with embedded crypto meeting these parameters is classified 5A002.a.

This is a short-range 2.4 GHz Bluetooth Low Energy wireless transceiver module for consumer/commercial connectivity. As mass-market wireless communications equipment not employing controlled encryption beyond standard implementations, it falls under Category 5 Part 1 telecommunications. Standard BLE radio modules with frequency-hopping/spread-spectrum at 2.4 GHz are classified under 5A991 (telecommunications equipment not controlled by 5A001), specifically 5A991.b for equipment using digital techniques.

item_name: Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 compute module (2 GB / 16 GB)
reasoning: This is a general-purpose, low-performance commercial single-board/compute module (quad-core Cortex-A72 at 1.5 GHz). Mass-market computing hardware of this class does not meet the high-performance APP thresholds of 4A003 and is not specially designed for controlled applications. It falls under the catch-all for computers and assemblies in 4A994, though if it qualifies as mass-market it could be EAR99; absent that, 4A994 is the controlling CCL entry.

A general-purpose 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller running at 168 MHz is a standard commercial embedded processor. It does not meet the high-performance ADP thresholds (e.g., APP) or specialized criteria of Category 3 (3A001) or Category 4 (4A003), nor any other CCL entry. Common commercial microcontrollers of this class are not specifically controlled and are classified EAR99.

A standard 1-inch broadband dielectric mirror for visible (400-750 nm) beam steering does not meet the technical thresholds of 6A004 optical mirrors (e.g., deformable/adaptive optics, large dimensions, special figure tolerances, or specific high-energy/laser-damage thresholds). A common visible turning mirror on fused silica is not described by any CCL entry and is classified EAR99.

A passive mechanical kinematic mirror mount is a general-purpose optomechanical component for holding/aligning optics. It is not an optical element, laser, or sensor controlled under Category 6, nor does it meet any positioning/precision tooling thresholds of Category 2. Such opto-mechanical hardware is not described by any specific CCL entry and is therefore EAR99.

A single-core ARM Cortex-A8 general-purpose applications microprocessor at 1.0 GHz does not meet the high-performance ('Adjusted Peak Performance') thresholds or specialized criteria of 3A001.a.1 or the microprocessor controls in Category 3/4. Standard commercial embedded processors of this class are not specifically described on the CCL and fall under EAR99.
